figures.gloss: The Northern Paiute mystic and prophet who catalyzed the 1890 Ghost Dance. Entering a deep trance during a solar eclipse, he journeyed to the spirit world and returned with a divine mandate: live in peace, dance the round dance, and await the imminent resurrection of the dead and regeneration of the earth.
figures.facet: He radically synthesized indigenous Basin weather-shamanism with Protestant eschatology, offering an apocalypse that demanded absolute pacifism and pan-tribal unity, explicitly rejecting armed resistance in favor of cosmic deliverance.
figures.provenance: James Mooney, 'The Ghost-dance Religion and the Sioux Outbreak of 1890' (1896); Michael Hittman, 'Wovoka and the Ghost Dance' (1990).

figures.gloss: A continuous, hypnotic circle dance lasting up to five days. Men and women with interlocked hands shuffled rhythmically, singing revealed songs to induce ecstatic trances, commune with the dead, and hasten the promised world renewal.
figures.facet: Unlike traditional dances meant to secure local game or harvests, this was a pan-Indian mechanism of cosmic acceleration—a physical ritual designed to wind the world's clock forward to the apocalypse.
figures.provenance: James Mooney, 'The Ghost-dance Religion and the Sioux Outbreak of 1890' (1896).

figures.gloss: The prophetic vision of a regenerated world, often conceptualized as a new, pure crust of earth sliding rapidly over the old. It was prophesied to bury all colonizers while restoring the depleted buffalo herds, wild grasses, and primeval ecological order.
figures.facet: Salvation was not conceptualized as an ascent to a distant heaven, but as a localized, geological reboot of the American landscape, physically burying the colonial trauma.
figures.provenance: Russell Thornton, 'We Shall Live Again: The 1870 and 1890 Ghost Dance Movements as Demographic Revitalization' (1986).

figures.gloss: The deceased Indigenous ancestors, prophesied to return to the world of the living alongside the regenerated earth. In trances, dancers saw them riding horses and driving vast herds of resurrected game toward the camps.
figures.facet: The dead are not passive residents of an underworld, but the active vanguard of the apocalypse, crossing the temporal veil to reclaim their stolen physical geography.
figures.provenance: James Mooney, 'The Ghost-dance Religion' (1896); Jeffrey Ostler, 'The Plains Sioux and U.S. Colonialism' (2004).

figures.gloss: A sacred garment, primarily adopted by the Lakota, constructed of muslin or buckskin and vividly painted with visionary symbols like eagles, stars, and buffalo. Consecrated through ritual, it was believed to render the wearer impervious to bullets.
figures.facet: It represents a militant adaptation of Wovoka's originally pacifist doctrine, transforming a movement of spiritual endurance into a theology of active, magically-protected resistance against the U.S. military.
figures.provenance: James Mooney, 'The Ghost-dance Religion' (1896); Raymond J. DeMallie, 'The Sixth Grandfather' (1984).

figures.gloss: The Northern Paiute prophet of the earlier 1870 Ghost Dance. Amidst severe epidemics and starvation, he received visions that the world would end and the dead would return via a train, establishing the foundational theology of crisis-response.
figures.facet: His prophecies were immediate, highly localized responses to demographic collapse, introducing the round dance as an emergency spiritual technology decades before it went pan-Indian.

figures.gloss: The supreme creator encountered by Wovoka in the spirit world. A syncretic fusion of the Christian God and traditional Paiute high deities, he is the architect of the coming renewal who orchestrates the moral laws of the dance.
figures.facet: He is a creator who actively changes his covenant: having turned his face away from the white man due to their crucifixion of his son, he transfers divine favor exclusively to Indigenous peoples.
figures.provenance: James Mooney, 'The Ghost-dance Religion' (1896); Michelene E. Pesantubbee, 'Religious Syncretism in the Ghost Dance' (1993).

figures.gloss: The figure of Jesus Christ, co-opted and radically reinterpreted. Wovoka was often identified by followers as Christ returned to earth, bearing the scars of the crucifixion, arriving to save Native peoples after European society rejected him.
figures.facet: Christ is radically decolonized. His murder by Europeans invalidates their spiritual authority entirely, transforming him into a vengeful liberator acting strictly on behalf of the colonized.

edges.note: Intra-tradition bond: The predecessor prophet of 1870 and his world-altering successor in 1890.
Intra-tradition bond: Wovoka's conscious embodiment of, and identification with, the returning Christ.
Intra-tradition bond: The specific ritual mechanism designed to literally pull the new world into physical reality.
Intra-tradition bond: The militant, protective adaptation of the shirt inverts the explicitly pacifist teachings of the original dance.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Wovoka explicitly identified himself, and was identified by Plains followers, as the returned Christ.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The final world-savior who leads humanity into the ultimate renovation of the earth and the resurrection of the dead.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An apocalyptic, physical renovation of the universe that permanently purges evil and restores a perfect, primeval state.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The living Earth conceptualized as an entity capable of violently throwing off its abusers to heal itself.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Direct incorporation of the Protestant Christian God into the Paiute vision of the supreme creator.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Explicit theological appropriation of the Christian savior, conceptually weaponized against his European worshippers.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A communal, rhythmic round dance designed to induce ecstatic trance, heal the community, and bridge the physical and spirit worlds.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Ancestors conceptualized not as distant spirits, but as an active, approaching force vital to the survival of the living.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The collective, venerated dead who maintain an active, protective presence in the affairs of their descendants.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Manufactured objects ritually charged with spirit power to provide absolute physical protection and repel enemy weaponry.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A messianic figure of the colonized whose return is prophesied to overturn the colonial order and restore indigenous sovereignty.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Paiute circle dance was adopted by Plains tribes and seamlessly synthesized with the existing sacred geometry of the hoop.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A syncretic prophet who receives direct revelation and merges indigenous belief systems with imported eschatology.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The longing for a return to an uncorrupted, pre-fall ecological paradise brimming with spontaneous abundance.
GOLD SEAM: Nineteenth-century American millenarian prophets whose date-specific hope of a transformed world survived disconfirmation by reinterpretation rather than collapse.

Colonial-era prophets whose healing revivals were answered with state suppression — mass deportations in the Congo, massacre at Wounded Knee — the same collision of indigenous prophecy and settler power.
Worn, blessed regalia carrying protection for the body of the believer — the badge and the shirt as membership and armor in one garment.

Prophets of dispossessed peoples inside settler states, visions turned into new liturgies in the same generation — Te Kooti's faith survived armed pursuit into a living church, where the Ghost Dance was broken at Wounded Knee.

dossier.essence: A cluster of Native American prophetic revitalization movements of the late 19th-century American West, centered on a circle dance believed to hasten world-renewal and the return of the dead. Two waves are distinguished: the 1870 Ghost Dance of the Northern Paiute prophet Wodziwob, and the far larger 1890 movement of the Northern Paiute prophet Wovoka (Jack Wilson), which spread across the Great Basin and Plains and became entangled, among the Lakota, with the Wounded Knee Massacre of December 29, 1890.
dossier.cosmos: Wovoka's revelation, received in a trance during an 1889 solar eclipse, promised an imminent renewal of the earth in which the Indigenous dead would return, the game (above all the buffalo) would be restored, and a purified world would replace the present one, while adherents lived peaceably and danced. It fused Great Basin shamanic tradition with Christian eschatology — Wovoka was identified by some followers with a returned Christ, and the creator with the Christian 'Father'. The renewal was imagined as a physical, this-worldly transformation of the American land rather than an ascent to heaven.
dossier.practice: The core rite was the round dance — participants joining hands in a circle and shuffling and singing revealed songs for days at a time to induce visionary trance and contact with the dead. Wovoka's original teaching was explicitly pacifist; as it spread to the Plains, some Lakota adopted the 'Ghost Shirt', believed to stop bullets, giving the movement a militant cast that alarmed U.S. authorities and helped precipitate the killing at Wounded Knee.
dossier.canon: Oral — Wovoka's teachings, carried by delegations and by letters (the so-called Messiah Letter), and the large corpus of Ghost Dance songs; the indispensable early record is the ethnographer James Mooney's firsthand study for the Bureau of American Ethnology.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds religion as the response to catastrophe — a prophetic movement that met colonial dispossession and demographic collapse with a danced demand for the renewal of the world and the return of the dead.
sources: James Mooney, 'The Ghost-Dance Religion and the Sioux Outbreak of 1890' (1896)
Michael Hittman, 'Wovoka and the Ghost Dance' (1990)
Gregory E. Smoak, 'Ghost Dances and Identity: Prophetic Religion and American Indian Ethnogenesis in the Nineteenth Century' (2006)
